summary: The author achieved 9th place in a bird audio classification competition by implementing a two-stage semi-supervised pipeline that leverages pre-trained SED and CNN architectures. They applied targeted audio and spectrogram augmentations, RMS sampling, and Exponential Moving Average (EMA) during training, followed by pseudo-label generation on 10-second chunks to boost performance. Key insights included the critical impact of random seed diversity on ensemble scores, the necessity of retaining 50% human voice rather than removing it entirely, and the discovery that multi-threaded ONNX inference caused timeouts. The final submission relied on weighted averaging of seed-diverse models with careful post-processing threshold smoothing and frequency cutoff adjustments.
extensive_summary:
## Dataset & Preprocessing
The solution utilized only `train_audio` and `train_soundscapes` from the 2025 competition. A crucial preprocessing step involved removing exactly 50% of human voice from the audio, as the author found that stripping all human voice degraded model performance.

## Stage 1 Training
The pipeline combined SED and CNN models. For raw signal augmentation, the author applied PitchShift, Shift, and Sumix (0.5 probability). Mel-spectrogram augmentations included Mixup2, Time masking, FilterAugment (0.5 probability), FrequencyMasking (0.5 probability), and PinkNoise (0.5 probability). Mel-spectrogram parameters were explicitly configured using torchaudio (SR=32000, n_fft=2048, n_mels=256/128, f_min=20, f_max=16000/14000, dynamic hop_length). RMS sampling was found superior to random sampling. EMA was applied throughout training to improve robustness and narrow epoch performance gaps. Backbones included `efficientnetv2_b3`, `eca_nfet_l0`, and `seresnext26t` for SED models (10s segments), and `efficientnet_b0` for CNN models (5s segments). Loss functions were FocalBCE for SED models, and a hybrid CE+BCE strategy for CNN models (CE early for convergence speed, BCE late).

## Stage 2 Pseudo-labeling
Models generated pseudo-labels on 10-second chunks. Only the top 10% of classes per sample were retained as soft labels, with others zeroed out. The same pipeline was retrained, selecting `efficientnetv2_b3` and `seresnext26t` as final backbones, yielding a >0.02 LB boost.

## Inference & TTA
10s chunks were fed into models with a 2-second window for TTA. All models were converted to ONNX format. The author noted that multi-threaded ONNX inference caused timeouts despite appearing viable, requiring single-threaded execution for successful submission.

## Ensemble & Post-processing
Weighted averaging was used for ensembling. Post-processing included smoothing predictions with `[0.2, 0.6, 0.2]` weights (boosting 0.005~0.01) and setting `fmax` to 2000 during inference (boosting 0.002). Random seed selection proved highly impactful, necessitating ensemble diversity across seeds.

## Failed Ideas & Insights
Several concepts that failed on the public leaderboard actually improved private scores, including resampling pseudo-labels by probability and adjusting probabilities by max/mean. Explicitly failed attempts included raw signal models, rank-average ensembling, RMS sampling with energy weights, using common names as auxiliary targets, lower rank power postprocessing, and adding Gaussian noise to raw signals.
competition_flow: Raw audio and soundscapes were preprocessed by removing 50% human voice, converted to Mel-spectrograms, and fed into a two-stage training pipeline using SED and CNN backbones with targeted audio augmentations and pseudo-labeling, followed by ONNX conversion, TTA, and weighted ensemble with threshold smoothing for final submission.
data_processing: Converted raw signals to Mel-spectrograms using torchaudio transforms (SR=32000, n_fft=2048, n_mels=256/128, f_min=20, f_max=16000/14000, dynamic hop_length)
Applied RMS sampling over random sampling
Converted models to ONNX format for inference
Applied TTA with 2-second window on 10s chunks
ensembling: Weighted average was used to ensemble models trained with different random seeds, with no extensive threshold tuning or calibration beyond prediction smoothing and frequency cutoff adjustments.
insights: RMS sampling outperformed random sampling for audio data.
Applying EMA during training improved model robustness and reduced performance variance across epochs.
Random seed selection had a surprisingly large impact on final scores, necessitating ensemble diversity across seeds.
Converting models to ONNX with multi-threading caused inference timeouts, requiring single-threaded execution for successful submission.
critical_findings: Removing all human voice from audio degraded model performance, so only 50% was removed instead.
Ideas that failed on the public leaderboard (like resampling pseudo-labels by probability and adjusting probabilities by max/mean) actually improved private leaderboard scores.
Multi-threaded ONNX inference led to timeouts despite appearing viable, revealing a hidden deployment bug.
what_did_not_work: use common name as auxiliary target
add guassian noise into raw signal
